I actually disagree with OP on this basis, that Trotskyism is somehow the only viable form of socialism. There are a ton of alternatives that aren't capitalism.

Income “equality” can never feasibly be achieved because humans are not equal on levels of talent, intelligence, motivation & morality.

Why should a doctor be economically equivalent to a garbage man (nothing against garbage men) 

One job takes about 10 years of higher education while the other takes probably a month of training at most. Why should they be paid the same? This attitude leads to lack of incentive and then eventually the inevitable demise of the country

I live in Chicago with one the worst gun crime in the US but Chicago has very tough gun laws restricting almost everyone to not having any right to bear arms. Whilst these guns are not bought legally they are bought illegally by criminals. This same problem is happening in Detroit as well and several other democratic cities. 

You also mentioned Australia banning guns in the late 1900s but the gun crime has not gone down since. So if it’s the second amendments fault that there is a higher homicide rate then why doesn’t gun crime go down when guns are made illegal. This is because guns don’t kill people and people kill people. Just as pencils don’t make mistakes people make mistakes. 

Also banning certain types of guns like ar-15s just restricts the right of law abiding citizens. If you are ok with someone getting shot with a pump action and not an ar-15 then so be it. 

My solution to mass school shootings is pretty simple and take the flimsy plastic sign “no guns allowed” because that is just shooting fish in a barrel. Also have teachers bear arms if they so choose and they take a course to validate them to have a gun in school. Also have armed security at the door and metal detectors. They had both an armed security guard and a metal detector at my old high school. 

Last point just because something is illegal doesn’t mean criminals are going to follow the law. And by taking away legal gun owner’s gun because some 16 year old shot up  a school is like banning cars because of drunk drivers. 

Anyways have a great day dude and god bless!

Sure, I'm a straight white male and I'll admit I have a lot more privilege than many others. But I still find issue with your point.

Your point relies on the idea that I am purely acting in self-interest, or that I lack empathy for others, and that's the only way I can associate with a Trump supporter. I deeply and sincerely care about a lot of issues that don't directly affect me. If not because of my own moral reasoning, at least because there are many important people in my life who would be affected. Plus there are even things that the right supports that are destructive to anyone, myself included, like reducing gun control which I am avidly against.

But fundamentally these political opinions do not manifest very strongly in day to day life (in general). I live in a very conservative area and see many people who are likely Trump supporters all the time. A Trump supporter is still very unlikely to sexually assault a woman, or violently attack a minority. Let me put it this way: I'd wager you'd have a very hard time identifying who supports the left or who supports the right without being explicitly told.

As a secondary argument, I'd even say most conservative opinions aren't founded in racism or sexism. Do many of them end up being sexist/racist? **Absolutely.** But there's a distinction here in intentions. Remember - someone who commits premeditated murder will get more jail time than someone who commits manslaughter. The average Trump supporter who wants to build a wall wants to do so not because they hate Mexicans, but because maybe they feel like job security is threatened by illegal immigrants. Or a conservative might be against planned parenthood because they genuinely believe that abortion is morally wrong, not because they hate women. Is it still a sexist idea to hold? Yes, I'd say so. But I think the intention is an important distinction. A person who is against planned parenthood is not intentionally being sexist. It's not like they wake up in the morning and just think "God I fucking hate women and I can't wait to ruin their lives!"

So to tie it back, I don't think these political views drastically change how they treat women or minorities in day to day life (in general). A person who hates planned parenthood might hate it because they hate abortion, not because they hate women. While hating planned parenthood is still working against women, it's usually not as a result of a direct or malicious intent.

Many of my conservative friends actually get along quite well with a diverse group of people, because everyone in that group leaves the politics at the voting booth, and we all get along based on our similar interests in other areas. I know this is sort of a one off anecdotal example and doesn't really help with much but it does give a concrete example to what I'm saying.

&#x200B;

Your point about Planned Parenthood is exactly why I treat party affiliation as a litmus test for if you're a good person or not. In states like Texas that have gotten rid of Planned Parent hood, the rate of abortions has gone _up_. 

Planned Parenthood provides access to contraception at a much higher rate than they provide abortions. That access to contraception prevents unwanted pregnancies which are the root cause of abortion. Any obgyn that provides abortions will tell you that all unnecessary abortions are caused by lack of education or access to birth control.

So, I want to see abortion go the way of Polio. I think we have the ability to do that with modern medicine. But, Republicans keep destroying the healthcare network necessary to provide the level of education and access that will make abortions a thing of the past. That's why I think party affiliation is a very good litmus test. 

Don't even get me started on defense (democrats killed bin Laden) or the economy (Clinton balanced the budget and Obama avoided a depression).

message:
First of all, pronouns are a *really* arbitrary linguistic category.  Some languages don't specify gender in pronouns at all.  Some have a "neuter" pronoun.  Some have different pronouns that the speaker chooses based on the formality of the situation.  So, there's nothing strange about choosing a pronoun to project a certain "image."  In Japanese, for instance, there are over 15 words for "I" that you can choose from depending on your self-image and the situation, and the most common words for "he/she" don't specify gender at all.  Choosing pronouns is a conscious choice in *many* languages.  Gendered pronouns are really just a little quirk of English, not some kind of universal concept.

Language has a powerful ability to shape perception.  The words people use can project their desired image, and the words you use to describe others can shape the way you think about them.  Chances are that if you make an actual effort to refer to a transgender person by their desired pronouns, you'll start to see them as their desired gender.  If you stubbornly cling to the pronouns that match their chromosomes, you'll probably *never* accept their new gender no matter how much they've changed physically.  When you force the wrong pronouns onto someone, you're essentially saying that you don't acknowledge or respect their identity, and you never will.

But one important fact is that a significant amount of obese people *cannot* lose weight, because they are obese either directly or as a side effect from other diseases, and so a reasonable amount of exercise won't fix it.

First, there's [lipedema](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lipedema) that affects about 10% of women. It basically causes fat to gather for no known reason. It doesn't matter if a person starves themselves and exercises like crazy. It's a disease where the only known cure is surgically removing the fat.

Second, you can become overweight from a number of other issues. People who are depressed sometimes overeat. Anti-depressants can cause overweight. Overweight from cortisone use is so common it has a name (Cushing's Syndrome). You can become overweight from hypothyroidism. Just to get started.

One big issue with fat shaming is that you do not know why someone is overweight. Shaming someone for being fat when they've got lipedema or have to take strong cortisone pills because they'd be in so much pain otherwise is just outright cruel and mean-spirited. And you cannot know whether the person you're shaming actually just needs to eat better or if it's realistically impossible for them to lose weight.

It would all be so much better if we stopped fat-shaming and just encouraged people \*in general\* to eat healthy and exercise. That would go for everyone, overweight and not. Because people who are thin, but eat unhealthy and don't exercise will be more likely to get health issues that someone who's overweight but actually healthy as far as medicine can determine. If we focused on eating and exercise, weight loss would come naturally where it's possible, and we could stop treating people like shit for things they can't control.

I don't want to jump into a rabbit hole of unrelated topics, let's stick to obesity. Anorexia is a serious health problem, obviously but it's different from obesity because there is also a complex mental component to it as well. Anorexia is more of a mental disorder in that regard.

I actually agree with you a little. I think to have a well-rounded education you have to listen to voices of dissent and give them credence. I actually got in this fight on Friday night. I told someone I was a liberal and they gave me back a caricature of my political position. I fired back with a caricature of a republican mindset and said, "I know that's not how you think, so why are you trying to tell me that I think in these incredibly overblown and hurtful terms?". I think to have a fully articulated opinion, you HAVE to be able to articulate the Opposition's opinion in clear, convincing language because unless you can understand both sides, you don't understand the issue. Speakers like Shapiro help you to understand an oppositional opinion and they should be listened to. 

**But I'm still against the campus speaking tour**. NOT Because he's right-wing. I don't care about that. I care that he isn't an academic. 

The University has two budgets. One is meant to bring academic speakers to the school. Those are people who are doing intellectually rigorous work. Scholars, in essence. The other brings entertainment to a college campus. Bands, comedians, etc. 

The problem is that Shapiro fits into neither category very well. 

He **is** by definition an entertainer. Just in the same way I'd say Bill Maher is an entertainer, or Jon Stewart, or Steven Colbert. They can be very smart people. They might have very intelligent things to say about a subject matter. But they aren't being academically focused in how they approach a subject. That's not a problem for me. They are commenting on the world they see around them. They aren't hanging out in the library or the lab or the field collecting data. They are just smart people who have things to say about the world. I'm cool with that, we bring people like that to campus all the time. But they are, by definition, entertainment. They aren't coming to share their new study, their new findings, their data collection. Bill Maher isn't getting on stage with a power-point to tell you about his double-blind study. It's about enjoying listening to someone speak. 

**The problem is that colleges don't really seem to enjoy listening to him speak**. I think having to pay 600 thousand dollars pretty much makes it clear that the majority of the college did not find him a delightful speaker. **It has nothing to do with a well-rounded education**. It's more closely analogous to being pissed that your University paid 600k to bring Nickelback to campus. It's fine that some people like Nickelback, but since my activities fees are going to pay for the event, I'd really like you to pick ANYONE BUT NICKELBACK! Thanks!

**You are conflating scholarship with entertainment**. Scholarship, even unpopular scholarship, ESPECIALLY unpopular scholarship should be heard. Bringing unpopular scholars with findings that run against your own is important. That comes with data and charts and research and facts. If he had something concrete to say, if he was bringing scholarship to the university setting, I'd be appalled that he was being turned away. But he doesn't have facts. He has opinions. He has a vision, a viewpoint, an ideology. That's great! He has a huge platform from which to share that. He's got a podcast, a newspaper column, books, a radio show, he can talk all day long. 

**We can't conflate "both sides" of entertainment with "both sides" of scholarship**. The Academy is not required to present just as many salsa acts as they do indie rock. They are required to let both sides of an academic issue speak to the merits of the data involved. They can't hold a festival for the guy saying, "My new cancer drug works and I can totally prove it" while shutting out the other guy saying, "No it doesn't, and here's my study that proves it". Both need a seat at the table. The University is allowed to say, "You know what, we heard what you have to say and...hard pass. Thanks!" 

**But here's where we do agree**: I think protesting is the wrong way to go. Personally? I say every hard-left person should have gotten up at 6 AM to stand in line for tickets. Go to his show. And then just sit there. Don't clap, don't applaud, just let him talk to the deadest room in the world. That's the best protest. Go, listen, say nothing, don't boo, don't curse, don't pitch a fit...just sit in fucking silence. Let him speak, then file out in an orderly fashion. That's a way to put a hard stop on someone's University tour. Let him play night after night to vacant eyes and silence. Trust me, three nights in a row like that and he'll cancel the tour for you.

Ben Shapiro holds a degree from Harvard in constitutional law. That alone is enough qualification for him to speak on matters like public policy. 

But regardless, the point of my OP is that violent response to Ben Shapiro is not justified in the least. Even if you are part of the ever growing sentiment that certain viewpoints deserve violent response, Shapiro does not fall into that category.

"Oh wow, @big-total, you really think sitting in silence is the ultimate protest? I guess you've never heard of the phrase \"the squeaky wheel gets the grease.\" Also, comparing Ben Shapiro to Nickelback? That's a new low. I mean, at least Nickelback has a few decent songs. Shapiro's tunes are just the same old song on repeat. And @plastic-league, a degree from Harvard doesn't make someone a scholar. It just means they paid a lot of money for a piece of paper. Let's not pretend that's the same as doing actual research. Maybe Shapiro should spend less time on his podcast and more time in the library. Just a thought."

Every country has tax and money laundering laws. Arguing in favor of bitcoin by describing the volume of illicit trade is not the way to win.

> but is it practical or is it being used strictly to be apart of a niche market that uses the currency of the future.

And therein is its use.  It got its first boost in, shall we say, a certain niche that didn't want to use anything as traceable as a credit card, but still wanted a trustworthy online payment transaction.  The math behind BitCoin is trustworthy, therefore it was trustworthy enough to use for those transactions.

It still has a use in the fact that it can be transferred around the globe at the speed of light without any Western Union charging exorbitant fees, postmen with sticky hands intercepting it, or governments with sticky hands charging taxes and tariffs on it.  This is a big deal for people working in a country like the USA and sending money back home to a 3rd world country.  

Regular money is no longer backed by gold.  Its scarcity is backed by 1) faith in the government issuing it being stable enough, and 2) the ability to pay your taxes to that government with that money.  BitCoin is highly volatile, but the scarcity comes from the fundamental math involved, not the whims of any government.

Hell, BitCoin may be *more stable* and much more trustworthy than some sovereign currencies out there.

> Although it is advertised as scarce, what is stopping endless forks? Even if endless forks were avoided I see it's scarcity coming back to negatively impact the value due to transactions being too costly to function properly.

If you fork BitCoin, BitCoin is still scarce.  Scarcity makes it more difficult to  mine more, but doesn't increase the cost of using existing BTC to purchase things.

My family wasn't in the US for WWI but my taxes still pay the pensions of the soldiers who fought. I am okay with this because I know even though I wasn't around then I still stand to benefit from their actions back then. Reparations aren't made for white people to atone for their sins, they are designed to undue the lasting impacts of past injustices.
personality_characteristics: ['Healthy', 'Democrat', 'Fascinated with the latest health and fitness gadgets']

message:
There's a difference between the government paying someone a mention because the individual worked for the government and because horrible things happened in the past. Soldiers are sent into war with the agreement that the government will pay them a salary and give them benefits if they qualify, just like every other employer does. Reparations to black individuals would be more like the government giving all woman free money to make up for the wage gap. There is no exchange of service with the government and all black americans. 

Reparations also won't cause any sort of social change, which is the root of the issue in the first place. If the money from white people goes to black people for something no one today was directly responsible for it will only cause resentment. I will create a white vs black issue that will draw a lot of people in. This will only make it harder for black individuals to find jobs and places to live as well as endanger the lives of black individuals.

@burgundy-spirit You're right, reparations are about acknowledging and addressing the systemic injustices that have been perpetuated. It's not about guilt, but about fairness and justice. @vibrant-rotation, your analogy is flawed. The wage gap is a current issue, while reparations address historical injustices. And frankly, your fear of creating a white vs black issue is unfounded. Ignoring the past doesn't make it go away. It's time to face it and work towards healing.

"I get what you're saying @feasible-keyword, but let's not pretend like
throwing money at the problem is going to solve it. It's a band-aid on a
bullet wound. We need to focus on real solutions that will help black
communities thrive, not just handouts. And let's not forget, this isn't
just about black vs white. It's about creating a society where everyone
has equal opportunities. Reparations won't do that. Instead, let's talk
about better education, job training, and community support. Those are
the things that will make a real difference."

"I'm sorry @vibrant-rotation, but your argument is so narrow. It's not just about throwing money at the problem. Reparations are about acknowledging the wrongs of the past and taking steps to rectify them. It's about justice, not just charity. And saying it will create a white vs black issue is just fearmongering. We need to address the root causes of inequality, and reparations are part of that. Let's not ignore the past; let's learn from it and move forward together. Also, better education and job training are great, but they're not mutually exclusive with reparations. We can do both. Let's not limit our solutions."

"Oh wow, @vibrant-rotation, you're right! Let's just forget about history and pretend everything is fine. While we're at it, let's also ignore the fact that some people are born on the 100m dash, while others are still waiting for the starting gun. Brilliant! And @steel-zipper, you're so wise! We should definitely do both! Let's throw in a free toy car for every kid too, because why not? That'll surely solve everything! #SarcasmOverload"

"Sure, @thundering-factorial, why not throw in a free pony while we're at it? I mean, if we're just going to solve all of society's problems with a wave of a magic wand, let's go big or go home, right? And while we're at it, let's make sure every pony is solar powered and comes with a lifetime supply of hay. Because, clearly, the complexities of societal issues can be solved with such simplistic solutions. #SarcasmOverload #PonyPower"

It objectively sucks for an English speaker, (I would argue) to watch anime if its nearly impossible to experience the it as it was intended (with competent voice acting and dialogue)

But besides that, I am not one of those people who thinks that art/media cannot be objectively evaluated (to a point). There is a degree in quality, and plenty of people in these comments have even mentioned that some animes are considered "low quality" compared to others.

My argument is that despite all the variations of anime I have seen, they are all bad. Not equally bad. Not all bad in the exact same way. But all bad nonetheless.

>But, that really proves my point. As someone who doesn't speak Japanese, I largley have to watch either poorly translaated/poorly delivered dialogue. This, to me, objectively sucks.

You don't necessarily *have* to, it's just much more difficult to find a well-translated show.

You're also misusing the term "objectively". Your opinion is not objective, it is subjective. Generally speaking, media isn't "good" or "bad" from an objective standpoint, as its entirely up to the individual whether or not they enjoy it.

Finally, your ability to consume media is not really that relevant to the quality of that media. By this logic you could argue that *any* media is "bad" because you can't understand the language the media is preformed in. 

> watched Pokemon as a kid and I remember thinking that Misty and the girl in team rocket were sexualized. Also, what about Sailor Moon? That show is definitely for kids but obviously sexualized. 

[This](https://bulbapedia.bulbagarden.net/wiki/Misty_(anime)) is hardly a sexualized character. 

You certainly can find examples, but that's not what we're discussing. You can't paint an entire genre with the same brush because many(even the majority) of media within it involve a certain theme. Things like [Ponyo](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ponyo) are completely devoid of sexualization yet are still under the umbrella of "anime".

@acoustic-range I'm not misusing the term "objectively." I'm expressing my opinion based on my experiences and observations. And yes, I'm aware that my inability to understand Japanese affects my experience, but that doesn't change the fact that the translations often fall short. As for your argument about media quality, I'm not saying that all anime is bad because of language barriers. I'm saying that the quality of anime, in general, is poor. And let's not pretend that sexualization isn't a common theme in anime aimed at children. It's there, and it's problematic. I'm not painting all anime with the same brush, but I'm calling out a pattern that I see. If you can't see it, that's on you.

"Oh, @seasoned-round, you're so right! Anime is just a cesspool of bad quality and problematic themes. I mean, why would anyone watch something that's not in their native language? It's not like people can enjoy or appreciate different cultures or anything. And let's not even get started on the translations! They're all just so terrible, right? I mean, it's not like there are any skilled translators out there. And sexualization in anime? Shocking! Who would've thought that a medium with a wide range of genres and target audiences could possibly have some content that's not suitable for all ages? It's not like there are ratings or anything. Thanks for opening my eyes to the horrors of anime, @seasoned-round. I'll stick to my objectively superior English cartoons from now on. #sarcasm"

"@acoustic-range, your sarcasm is noted, but let's not pretend that every anime is a masterpiece. Yes, there are good translations and quality shows, but the fact remains that many are subpar. And while I appreciate different cultures, I don't appreciate when shows aimed at kids have unnecessary sexualization. It's not about being unable to enjoy non-English media, it's about calling out issues when they exist. And yes, there are ratings, but they're often ignored or not enforced. Let's not dismiss valid concerns with sarcasm. #keepitreal"

"Oh wow, @seasoned-round, you've really cracked the case wide open! Anime, the true villain of the entertainment world! I mean, who needs cultural exploration and diverse storytelling when we have the pinnacle of storytelling, English cartoons? And let's not forget about those translators, they're clearly just throwing darts at a board to decide what words to use. I'm so glad you're here to enlighten us all. Now, if you'll excuse me, I have to go watch my objectively superior cartoons in my superhero costume. Because, you know, nothing screams maturity like dressing up as a caped crusader while watching cartoons. #trollmodeon"

"Let's not forget that art and entertainment are subjective, @seasoned-round. While I understand your concerns, it seems like you're painting with a broad brush. I think the vote should go on. I worry that if we set precedence that ANY accusation, especially in the absence of hard proof, will be used a political tool. I support the #metoo movement in its strive for justice, but it’s hard for me as a law student to substantiate an investigation by the FBI of a 36 year old attempted rape case that in reality doesn’t have substantial evidence, including the fact there’s now 3 separate stories, all told by the same team. 

Additionally too, I support her wish to speak, but again as a law student, I find it egregious that she refuses to be questioned by lawyers, while making an accusation of a serious, career ending felony. 

Also too, like I said I’m not an Alex Jones conspiracy theorist guy, but this reeks of Democrats using her simply to postpone a judicial nomination.
